sectiondef.header: Instances of the execution policy types

briefdescription.para: Tag used in algorithms for a sequenced_policy.
Tag used in algorithms for a parallel_policy.
Tag used in algorithms for a parallel_unsequenced_policy.
Tag used in algorithms for an unsequenced_policy.
Checks whether T is a standard or implementation-defined execution policy type.
Make an executor appropriate to a given policy and a pair of iterators This depends, of course, of the default executors we have available and.
The state owner got destroyed before the promise has been fulfilled.
Attempted to retrieve a unique future twice.
Promise has already been fulfilled.
There is no shared state we can access.
Invalid operation on deferred future.
Error codes for futures.
Default partitioner used by parallel algorithms.

detaileddescription.para: Its type and parameters might change

memberdef.type: std::conjunction< std::conditional_t< is_input_iterator_v< I >, std::true_type, std::false_type >, std::conditional_t< is_input_iterator_v< S >, std::true_type, std::false_type >, std::is_invocable< T, I, S > >
memberdef.definition: using futures::is_partitioner = typedef std::conjunction< std::conditional_t< is_input_iterator_v<I>, std::true_type, std::false_type>, std::conditional_t< is_input_iterator_v<S>, std::true_type, std::false_type>, std::is_invocable<T, I, S> >
memberdef.name: is_partitioner

briefdescription.para: Determine if P is a valid partitioner for the iterator range [I,S].
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 has-member-difference-type concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 has-member-element-type concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 "has-iterator-traits-difference-type" concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 "has-iterator-traits-iterator-category" concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 "has-iterator-traits-iterator-concept" concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 "has-iterator-traits-value-type" concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 has-member-value-type concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 assignable_from concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 constructible_from concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 convertible_to concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 copyable concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 default_initializable concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 derived_from concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 equality_comparable concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 equality_comparable_with concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 forward_iterator concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 incrementable concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 indirectly_binary_invocable concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 indirectly_readable concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 indirectly_unary_invocable concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 input_iterator concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 input_or_output_iterator concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 input_range concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 movable concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 move_constructible concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 partially_ordered_with concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 range concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 regular concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 semiregular concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 sentinel_for concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 subtractable concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 swappable concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 totally_ordered concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 totally_ordered_with concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 weakly_equality_comparable concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 weakly_incrementable concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 iter_concept concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 iter_difference concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 iter_reference concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 iter_rvalue_reference concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 iter_value concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 iterator_t concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 range_value concept.
A C++17 type trait equivalent to the C++20 remove_cvref concept.
The default execution context for async operations.

detaileddescription.para: Unless an executor is explicitly provided, this is the executor we use for async operations.

This is the ASIO thread pool execution context with a default number of threads. However, the default execution context (and its type) might change in other versions of this library if something more general comes along. As the standard for executors gets adopted, libraries are likely to provide better implementations.

Also note that executors might not allow work-stealing. This needs to be taken into account when implementing algorithms with recursive tasks. One common options is to use try_async for recursive tasks.

Also note that, in the executors notation, the pool is an execution context but not an executor:
Execution context: a place where we can execute functions
A thread pool is an execution context, not an executor

An execution context is:
Usually long lived
Non-copyable
May contain additional state, such as timers, and threads

briefdescription.para: Default executor type.
Determine if type is an executor.

detaileddescription.para: We only consider asio executors to be executors for now Future and previous executor models can be considered here, as long as their interface is the same as asio or we implement their respective traits to make async work properly.

briefdescription.para: A simple future type similar to std::future

detaileddescription.para: We should only use this future type for eager tasks that do not expect continuations.

memberdef.type: basic_future< T, future_options< executor_opt< Executor >, continuable_opt > >
memberdef.definition: using futures::cfuture = typedef basic_future< T, future_options<executor_opt<Executor>, continuable_opt> >
memberdef.name: cfuture

briefdescription.para: A future type with lazy continuations.

detaileddescription.para: Futures with lazy continuations contains a list of continuation tasks to be launched once the main task is complete.
This is what a futures::async returns by default when the first function parameter is not a futures::stop_token.

memberdef.type: basic_future< T, future_options< executor_opt< Executor >, continuable_opt, stoppable_opt > >
memberdef.definition: using futures::jcfuture = typedef basic_future< T, future_options<executor_opt<Executor>, continuable_opt, stoppable_opt> >
memberdef.name: jcfuture

briefdescription.para: A future type with lazy continuations and stop tokens.

detaileddescription.para: It's a quite common use case that we need a way to cancel futures and jcfuture provides us with an even better way to do that.
This is what futures::async returns when the first function parameter is a futures::stop_token

memberdef.type: basic_future< T, future_options< executor_opt< Executor >, always_deferred_opt > >
memberdef.definition: using futures::dfuture = typedef basic_future< T, future_options<executor_opt<Executor>, always_deferred_opt> >
memberdef.name: dfuture

briefdescription.para: A deferred future type.

detaileddescription.para: This is a future type whose main task will only be launched when we wait for its results from another execution context.
This is what the function schedule returns when the first task parameter is not a stop token.
The state of this future stores the function to be run.
This future type supports continuations without the continuation lists of continuable futures.

memberdef.type: basic_future< T, future_options< executor_opt< Executor >, always_deferred_opt > >
memberdef.definition: using futures::jdfuture = typedef basic_future< T, future_options<executor_opt<Executor>, always_deferred_opt> >
memberdef.name: jdfuture

briefdescription.para: A deferred stoppable future type.

detaileddescription.para: This is a future type whose main task will only be launched when we wait for its results from another execution context.
Once the task is launched, it can be requested to stop through its stop source.
This is what the function schedule returns when the first task parameter is a stop token.

briefdescription.para: A future that simply holds a ready value.

detaileddescription.para: This is the future type we use for constant values. This is the future type we usually return from functions such as make_ready_future.
These futures have no support for associated executors, continuations, or deferred tasks.
Like deferred futures, the operation state is stored inline.

memberdef.type: basic_future< T, future_options< executor_opt< Executor >, shared_opt > >
memberdef.definition: using futures::shared_future = typedef basic_future<T, future_options<executor_opt<Executor>, shared_opt> >
memberdef.name: shared_future

briefdescription.para: A simple std::shared_future.

detaileddescription.para: This is what a futures::future::share() returns

memberdef.type: basic_future< T, future_options< executor_opt< Executor >, continuable_opt, shared_opt > >
memberdef.definition: using futures::shared_cfuture = typedef basic_future< T, future_options<executor_opt<Executor>, continuable_opt, shared_opt> >
memberdef.name: shared_cfuture

briefdescription.para: A shared future type with lazy continuations.

detaileddescription.para: This is what a futures::cfuture::share() returns

memberdef.type: basic_future< T, future_options< executor_opt< Executor >, continuable_opt, stoppable_opt, shared_opt > >
memberdef.definition: using futures::shared_jcfuture = typedef basic_future< T, future_options< executor_opt<Executor>, continuable_opt, stoppable_opt, shared_opt> >
memberdef.name: shared_jcfuture

briefdescription.para: A shared future type with lazy continuations and stop tokens.

detaileddescription.para: This is what a futures::jcfuture::share() returns

memberdef.type: basic_future< T, future_options< executor_opt< Executor >, always_deferred_opt, shared_opt > >
memberdef.definition: using futures::shared_dfuture = typedef basic_future< T, future_options<executor_opt<Executor>, always_deferred_opt, shared_opt> >
memberdef.name: shared_dfuture

briefdescription.para: A shared future type with deferred task.

detaileddescription.para: This is what a futures::dfuture::share() returns

memberdef.type: basic_future< T, future_options< executor_opt< Executor >, stoppable_opt, always_deferred_opt, shared_opt > >
memberdef.definition: using futures::shared_jdfuture = typedef basic_future< T, future_options< executor_opt<Executor>, stoppable_opt, always_deferred_opt, shared_opt> >
memberdef.name: shared_jdfuture

briefdescription.para: A shared future type with deferred task and stop token.

detaileddescription.para: This is what a futures::jdfuture::share() returns

briefdescription.para: A shared future that simply holds a ready value.
Determine type a future object holds.

simplesect.para: Not to be confused with continuation unwrapping

briefdescription.para: Checks if a predicate is true for all the elements in a range.
Checks if a predicate is true for any of the elements in a range.
Returns the number of elements matching an element.
Returns the number of elements satisfying specific criteria.
Finds the first element equal to another element.
Finds the first element satisfying specific criteria.
Finds the first element not satisfying specific criteria.
Applies a function to a range of elements.
Checks if a predicate is true for none of the elements in a range.
Determine if P is a valid partitioner for the iterator range [I,S].
Sums up (or accumulate with a custom function) a range of elements, except out of order.
Determine if type is an executor.
Empty struct to initialize a stop_source without a shared stop state.
Customization point to determine if a type is a future type as a bool value.
Customization point to determine if a type is a shared future type.
Customization point to determine if a type is a shared future type.
Customization point to define future as supporting lazy continuations.
Customization point to define future as stoppable.
Customization point to define future having a common stop token.
Customization point to define future as always deferred.
Determine if a future type has an executor.
Create a proxy pair with a lambda and an executor.

detaileddescription.para: For this operation, we needed an operator with higher precedence than operator>> Our options are: +, -, *, /, %, &, !, ~. Although + seems like an obvious choice, % is the one that leads to less conflict with other functions.

simplesect.para: A proxy pair to schedule execution

briefdescription.para: Make a placeholder future object that is ready.

simplesect.para: A future associated with the shared state that is created.

briefdescription.para: Make a placeholder future object that is ready from a reference.

simplesect.para: A future associated with the shared state that is created.

briefdescription.para: Make a placeholder void future object that is ready.

simplesect.para: A future associated with the shared state that is created.

briefdescription.para: Make a placeholder future object that is ready with an exception from an exception ptr.

simplesect.para: A future associated with the shared state that is created.

briefdescription.para: Make a placeholder future object that is ready with from any exception.

simplesect.para: A future associated with the shared state that is created.

briefdescription.para: Schedule a continuation function to a future.

detaileddescription.para: This creates a continuation that gets executed when the before future is over. The continuation needs to be invocable with the return type of the previous future.

This function works for all kinds of futures but behavior depends on the input:
If previous future is continuable, attach the function to the continuation list
If previous future is not continuable (such as std::future), post to execution with deferred policy In both cases, the result becomes a cfuture or jcfuture.

Stop tokens are also propagated:
If after function expects a stop token:
If previous future is stoppable and not-shared: return jcfuture with shared stop source
Otherwise: return jcfuture with new stop source
If after function does not expect a stop token:
If previous future is stoppable and not-shared: return jcfuture with shared stop source
Otherwise: return cfuture with no stop source

simplesect.para: A continuation to the before future

briefdescription.para: Schedule a continuation function to a future with the default executor.

simplesect.para: A continuation to the before future

briefdescription.para: Operator to schedule a continuation function to a future.

simplesect.para: A continuation to the before future

briefdescription.para: Schedule a continuation function to a future with a custom executor.

simplesect.para: A continuation to the before future

memberdef.type: when_all_future< detail::small_vector< to_future_t< typename std::iterator_traits< InputIt >::value_type > > >
memberdef.name: when_all

briefdescription.para: Create a future object that becomes ready when the range of input futures becomes ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function does not participate in overload resolution unless InputIt's value type (i.e., typename std::iterator_traits<InputIt>::value_type) is a std::future or std::shared_future.
This overload uses a small vector for avoid further allocations for such a simple operation.

simplesect.para: Future object of type when_all_future

memberdef.type: when_all_future< detail::small_vector< to_future_t< typename std::iterator_traits< typename std::decay_t< Range >::iterator >::value_type > > >
memberdef.name: when_all

briefdescription.para: Create a future object that becomes ready when the range of input futures becomes ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function does not participate in overload resolution unless the range type is_future.

simplesect.para: Future object of type when_all_future

briefdescription.para: Create a future object that becomes ready when all of the input futures become ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function does not participate in overload resolution unless every argument is either a (possibly cv-qualified) shared_future or a cv-unqualified future, as defined by the trait is_future.

simplesect.para: Future object of type when_all_future

briefdescription.para: Operator to create a future object that becomes ready when all of the input futures are ready.

detaileddescription.para: Operator&& works for futures and functions (which are converted to futures with the default executor) If the future is a when_all_future itself, then it gets merged instead of becoming a child future of another when_all_future.
When the user asks for f1 && f2 && f3, we want that to return a single future that waits for <f1,f2,f3> rather than a future that wait for two futures <f1,<f2,f3>>.
This emulates the usual behavior we expect from other types with operator&&.

Note that this default behaviour is different from when_all(...), which doesn't merge the when_all_future objects by default, because they are variadic functions and this intention can be controlled explicitly:
when_all(f1,f2,f3) -> <f1,f2,f3>
when_all(f1,when_all(f2,f3)) -> <f1,<f2,f3>>

simplesect.para: when_all_future object that concatenates all futures

memberdef.type: when_any_future< detail::small_vector< to_future_t< typename std::iterator_traits< InputIt >::value_type > > >
memberdef.name: when_any

briefdescription.para: Create a future object that becomes ready when any of the futures in the range is ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function does not participate in overload resolution unless InputIt's value type (i.e., typename std::iterator_traits<InputIt>::value_type) is_future .
This overload uses a small vector to avoid further allocations for such a simple operation.

simplesect.para: when_any_future with all future objects

memberdef.type: when_any_future< detail::small_vector< to_future_t< typename std::iterator_traits< typename std::decay_t< Range >::iterator >::value_type > > >
memberdef.name: when_any

briefdescription.para: Create a future object that becomes ready when any of the futures in the range is ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function does not participate in overload resolution unless every argument is either a (possibly cv-qualified) std::shared_future or a cv-unqualified std::future.

simplesect.para: when_any_future with all future objects

briefdescription.para: Create a future object that becomes ready when any of the input futures is ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function does not participate in overload resolution unless every argument is either a (possibly cv-qualified) std::shared_future or a cv-unqualified std::future.

simplesect.para: when_any_future with all future objects

briefdescription.para: Operator to create a future object that becomes ready when any of the input futures is ready.

detaileddescription.para: ready operator|| works for futures and functions (which are converted to futures with the default executor) If the future is a when_any_future itself, then it gets merged instead of becoming a child future of another when_any_future.
When the user asks for f1 || f2 || f3, we want that to return a single future that waits for <f1 || f2 || f3> rather than a future that wait for two futures <f1 || <f2 || f3>>.
This emulates the usual behavior we expect from other types with operator||.

Note that this default behaviour is different from when_any(...), which doesn't merge the when_any_future objects by default, because they are variadic functions and this intention can be controlled explicitly:
when_any(f1,f2,f3) -> <f1 || f2 || f3>
when_any(f1,when_any(f2,f3)) -> <f1 || <f2 || f3>>

briefdescription.para: Determine a reasonable minimum grain size depending on the number of elements in a sequence.
Create an instance of the default partitioner with a reasonable grain size for n elements.

detaileddescription.para: The default partitioner type and parameters might change

param.type: std::enable_if_t< is_input_iterator_v< I > &&is_sentinel_for_v< S, I >, int >

briefdescription.para: Create an instance of the default partitioner with a reasonable grain for the range first, last

detaileddescription.para: The default partitioner type and parameters might change

briefdescription.para: Create an instance of the default partitioner with a reasonable grain for the range r

detaileddescription.para: The default partitioner type and parameters might change

briefdescription.para: A version of hardware_concurrency that always returns at least 1.

detaileddescription.para: This function is a safer version of hardware_concurrency that always returns at least 1 to represent the current context when the value is not computable.

listitem.para: It never returns 0, 1 is returned instead.
It is guaranteed to remain constant for the duration of the program.

detaileddescription.para: It also improves on hardware_concurrency to provide a default value of 1 when the function is being executed at compile time. This allows partitioners and algorithms to be constexpr.

simplesect.para: Number of concurrent threads supported. If the value is not well-defined or not computable, returns 1.

briefdescription.para: Create an instance of the default execution context.

simplesect.para: Reference to the default execution context for async

briefdescription.para: Create an Asio thread pool executor for the default thread pool.

detaileddescription.para:
In the executors notation:
Executor: set of rules governing where, when and how to run a function object
A thread pool is an execution context for which we can create executors pointing to the pool.
The executor rule for the default thread pool executor is to run function objects in the pool and nowhere else.

An executor is:
Lightweight and copyable (just references and pointers to the execution context).
May be long or short lived.
May be customized on a fine-grained basis, such as exception behavior, and order
There might be many executor types associated with with the same execution context.

simplesect.para: Executor handle to the default execution context

briefdescription.para: Get the inline execution context.
Make an inline executor object.
Make an new thread executor object.
Wait for future types and retrieve their values.

detaileddescription.para: This syntax is most useful for cases where we are immediately requesting the future result.
The function also makes the syntax optionally a little closer to languages such as javascript.

simplesect.para: This function only participates in overload resolution if all types are futures.
The result of the future object

briefdescription.para: Wait for future types and retrieve their values as a tuple.

simplesect.para: This function only participates in overload resolutions if all types are futures.
The result of the future object

briefdescription.para: Function to return a common reference to a global future error category.
Check if a future is ready.

detaileddescription.para: Although basic_future has its more efficient is_ready function, this free function allows us to query other futures that don't implement is_ready, such as std::future.

briefdescription.para: Launch an asynchronous task with the specified executor.

detaileddescription.para: This version of the async function will always use the specified executor instead of creating a new thread.
If no executor is provided, then the function is run in a default executor created from the default thread pool. The default executor also ensures the function will not launch one thread per task.
The task might accept a stop token as its first parameter, in which case the function returns a continuable and stoppable future type. Otherwise, this function returns a continuable future type.

simplesect.para: An eager future object whose shared state refers to the task result. The type of this future object depends on the task. If the task expects a stop_token, the future will return a continuable, stoppable, eager future. Otherwise, the function will return a continuable eager future.

briefdescription.para: Launch an asynchronous task with the default executor.

simplesect.para: A future object with the function results

briefdescription.para: Schedule an asynchronous task with the specified executor.

detaileddescription.para: This function schedules a deferred future. The task will only be launched in the executor when some other execution context waits for the value associated to this future.

simplesect.para: A deferred future object whose shared state refers to the task result. The type of this future object depends on the task. If the task expects a stop_token, the future will return a stoppable deferred future. Otherwise, the function will return a deferred future.

briefdescription.para: Schedule an asynchronous task with the default executor.

simplesect.para: A future object with the function results

briefdescription.para: Specializes the std::swap algorithm.
Swap the value of two promises.
Wait for a sequence of futures to be ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function waits for all futures in the range [first, last) to be ready. It simply waits iteratively for each of the futures to be ready.

simplesect.para: This function is adapted from boost::wait_for_all

parameterdescription.para: Iterator type in a range of futures
Iterator to the first element in the range
Iterator to one past the last element in the range

briefdescription.para: Wait for a sequence of futures to be ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function waits for all futures in the range r to be ready. It simply waits iteratively for each of the futures to be ready.

simplesect.para: This function is adapted from boost::wait_for_all

briefdescription.para: Wait for a sequence of futures to be ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function waits for all specified futures fs... to be ready.
It creates a compile-time fixed-size data structure to store references to all of the futures and then waits for each of the futures to be ready.

simplesect.para: This function is adapted from boost::wait_for_all

briefdescription.para: Wait for a sequence of futures to be ready.

memberdef.argsstring: (const std::chrono::duration< Rep, Period > &timeout_duration, Iterator first, Iterator last)
memberdef.name: wait_for_all_for

briefdescription.para: Wait for a sequence of futures to be ready.

parameterdescription.para: Iterator type in a range of futures
Iterator to the first element in the range
Iterator to one past the last element in the range

simplesect.para: std::future_status::ready if all futures got ready. std::future_status::timeout otherwise.

briefdescription.para: Wait for a sequence of futures to be ready.

simplesect.para: std::future_status::ready if all futures got ready. std::future_status::timeout otherwise.

briefdescription.para: Wait for a sequence of futures to be ready.

simplesect.para: std::future_status::ready if all futures got ready. std::future_status::timeout otherwise.

briefdescription.para: Wait for a sequence of futures to be ready.

simplesect.para: std::future_status::ready if all futures got ready. std::future_status::timeout otherwise.

memberdef.argsstring: (const std::chrono::time_point< Clock, Duration > &timeout_time, Iterator first, Iterator last)
memberdef.name: wait_for_all_until

briefdescription.para: Wait for a sequence of futures to be ready.

parameterdescription.para: Iterator type in a range of futures
Iterator to the first element in the range
Iterator to one past the last element in the range

simplesect.para: std::future_status::ready if all futures got ready. std::future_status::timeout otherwise.

briefdescription.para: Wait for a sequence of futures to be ready.

simplesect.para: std::future_status::ready if all futures got ready. std::future_status::timeout otherwise.

briefdescription.para: Wait for a sequence of futures to be ready.

simplesect.para: std::future_status::ready if all futures got ready. std::future_status::timeout otherwise.

briefdescription.para: Wait for a sequence of futures to be ready.

simplesect.para: std::future_status::ready if all futures got ready. std::future_status::timeout otherwise.

briefdescription.para: Wait for any future in a sequence to be ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function waits for any future in the range [first, last) to be ready.
Unlike wait_for_all, this function requires special data structures to allow that to happen without blocking.

For disjunctions, we have few options:
If the input futures support external notifiers:
Attach continuations to notify when a task is over
If the input futures do not have lazy continuations:
Polling in a busy loop until one of the futures is ready
Polling with exponential backoffs until one of the futures is ready
Launching n continuation tasks that set a promise when one of the futures is ready
Hybrids, usually polling for short tasks and launching threads for other tasks
If the input futures are mixed in regards to lazy continuations:
Mix the strategies above, depending on each input future
If the thresholds for these strategies are reasonable, this should be efficient for futures with or without lazy continuations.

simplesect.para: This function is adapted from boost::wait_for_any

parameterdescription.para: Iterator type in a range of futures
Iterator to the first element in the range
Iterator to one past the last element in the range

simplesect.para: Iterator to the first future that got ready

briefdescription.para: Wait for any future in a sequence to be ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function waits for any future in the range r to be ready. This function requires special data structures to allow that to happen without blocking.

simplesect.para: This function is adapted from boost::wait_for_any
Iterator to the first future that got ready

briefdescription.para: Wait for any future in a sequence to be ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function waits for all specified futures fs... to be ready.

simplesect.para: This function is adapted from boost::wait_for_any
Index of the first future that got ready

briefdescription.para: Wait for any future in a tuple to be ready.

detaileddescription.para: This function waits for all specified futures fs... to be ready.

simplesect.para: This function is adapted from boost::wait_for_any
Index of the first future that got ready

memberdef.argsstring: (const std::chrono::duration< Rep, Period > &timeout_duration, Iterator first, Iterator last)
memberdef.name: wait_for_any_for

briefdescription.para: Wait for any future in a sequence to be ready.

parameterdescription.para: Iterator type in a range of futures
Iterator to the first element in the range
Iterator to one past the last element in the range

simplesect.para: Iterator to the future which got ready

briefdescription.para: Wait for any future in a sequence to be ready.

parameterdescription.para: Iterator type in a range of futures

simplesect.para: Iterator to the future which got ready

briefdescription.para: Wait for any future in a sequence to be ready.

simplesect.para: Index of the future which got ready

briefdescription.para: Wait for any future in a sequence to be ready.

simplesect.para: Index of the future which got ready

memberdef.argsstring: (const std::chrono::time_point< Clock, Duration > &timeout_time, Iterator first, Iterator last)
memberdef.name: wait_for_any_until

briefdescription.para: Wait for any future in a sequence to be ready.

parameterdescription.para: Iterator type in a range of futures
Iterator to the first element in the range
Iterator to one past the last element in the range

simplesect.para: Iterator to the future which got ready

briefdescription.para: Wait for any future in a sequence to be ready.

simplesect.para: Iterator to the future which got ready

briefdescription.para: Wait for any future in a sequence to be ready.

simplesect.para: Index of the future which got ready

briefdescription.para: Wait for any future in a sequence to be ready.

simplesect.para: Index of the future which got ready

briefdescription.para: Because all tasks need to be done to achieve the result, the algorithm doesn't depend much on the properties of the underlying futures.

detaileddescription.para: The traits help us generate auxiliary algorithm overloads This is somewhat similar to the pattern of traits and algorithms for ranges and views It allows us to get algorithm overloads for free, including default inference of the best execution policies.
A partitioner is a light callable object that takes a pair of iterators and returns the middle of the sequence.
The thread that is awaiting just needs sleep and await for each of the internal futures.

The usual approach, without our future concepts, like in returning another std::future, is to start another polling thread, which sets a promise when all other futures are ready. If the futures support lazy continuations, these promises can be set from the previous objects. However, this has an obvious cost for such a trivial operation, given that the solutions is already available in the underlying futures.

Instead, we implement one more future type when_all_future that can query if the futures are ready and waits for them to be ready whenever get() is called. This proxy object can then be converted to a regular future if the user needs to.

This has a disadvantage over futures with lazy continuations because we might need to schedule another task if we need notifications from this future. However, we avoid scheduling another task right now, so this is, at worst, as good as the common approach of wrapping it into another existing future type.
If the input futures are not shared, they are moved into when_all_future and are invalidated, as usual. The when_all_future cannot be shared.
In particular, it returns an iterator middle that forms a subrange first/middle which the algorithm should solve inline before scheduling the subrange middle/last in the executor.
